serving.natural_language_portion: One typical piece of ham chim peng with red bean filling weighs approximately 80 g and is commonly consumed as a breakfast snack or morning tea item in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Ham Chim Peng (Red Bean) — Singapore HPB Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Ham Chim Peng with Red Bean (cr_id F11090021). All core macros and available micronutrients are taken from this canonical entry with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of deep-fried wheat dough products — analogous estimate based on fried dough (youtiao/Chinese cruller) GI data
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for ham chim peng with red bean exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 76 (high) based on the closest analogous food: deep-fried wheat dough (Chinese cruller / youtiao), which has a reported GI of approximately 74–76 in the University of Sydney GI Database. The addition of sweetened red bean paste increases the sugar load and is expected to maintain or slightly elevate the GI relative to plain fried dough. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (76 × 44.2) / 100 ≈ 33.6, rounded to 33.5 for an 80 g serving (carbohydrate_g per serving = 55.2 × 0.80 = 44.2 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 346 mg/100 g combined with high sugar load from sweetened red bean paste and saturated fat from deep-frying
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit ham chim peng with red bean due to its borderline sodium content (346 mg/100 g), high sugar load from sweetened red bean paste, and saturated fat from deep-frying, all of which compound cardiovascular risk; if consumed, limit to no more than one small piece on an occasional basis.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycaemic index (~76), high carbohydrate load (55.2 g/100 g), and estimated sugar ~18 g/100 g with low dietary fibre (2.2 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Ham chim peng with red bean is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its high GI (~76), high refined carbohydrate content (55.2 g/100 g), estimated sugar of approximately 18 g/100 g, and low dietary fibre (2.2 g/100 g), which together produce a very high glycaemic load (~33.5 per 80 g serving) and should be avoided in diabetes management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying increases saturated fat and potential trans fat exposure; high sugar and refined carbohydrate content elevates triglyceride and LDL risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid ham chim peng with red bean as deep-frying introduces saturated fat (estimated 2.1 g/100 g) and potential trans fats, while the high refined carbohydrate and sugar content (55.2 g carbohydrate, ~18 g sugar per 100 g) promotes dyslipidaemia and elevated triglycerides, compounding atherogenic risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs); high sugar content promotes systemic inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Ham chim peng with red bean should be avoided by individuals with rheumatoid arthritis as deep-frying generates pro-inflammatory advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), and the high sugar content from sweetened red bean paste (~18 g/100 g) further promotes inflammatory mediator activity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ham Chim Peng with Red Bean (咸煎饼（红豆）) is a popular Singaporean Chinese deep-fried snack of Cantonese origin, widely sold at hawker centres, wet markets, and traditional bakeries across Singapore and Malaysia. The snack consists of a leavened wheat dough disc that is deep-fried until golden and puffy, with a sweetened red bean paste filling in the centre. Despite its name meaning 'salty fried biscuit' in Cantonese, the red bean variant is notably sweet due to the sugared azuki bean paste filling, contrasting with the slightly savoury, chewy dough exterior. The texture is crisp on the outside and soft and doughy within, with a sweet, earthy red bean centre. Nutritionally, ham chim peng with red bean is energy-dense at 366 kcal per 100 g, with 55.2 g carbohydrate, 13.4 g fat, and 5.8 g protein per 100 g. The estimated sugar content is approximately 18 g/100 g from the sweetened red bean paste, while dietary fibre is low at 2.2 g/100 g. Sodium is 346 mg/100 g. The food has a high glycaemic index (~76) and a very high glycaemic load (~33.5 per 80 g serving), making it clinically unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. Deep-frying also generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), which are pro-inflammatory and of concern for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis. Ham chim peng is typically consumed as a breakfast item or morning snack, often paired with soy milk or kopi (local coffee). It is vegetarian, vegan, and generally considered halal in its traditional preparation.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F11090021) with a confidence of 0.95. The source is an approved primary regional source for Singaporean dishes. GI is estimated from the closest analogous food in the University of Sydney GI Database (deep-fried wheat dough) and is clearly documented as an analogous estimate. Sugar_g is estimated at 18 g/100 g based on the known composition of sweetened red bean paste and the overall carbohydrate profile; saturated_fat_g is estimated at 2.1 g/100 g based on typical deep-fried wheat dough profiles from USDA FoodData Central as a secondary reference.
notes: 1. sugar_g (18.0 g/100 g) is an estimate derived from the known sugar content of sweetened red bean paste and the total carbohydrate profile (55.2 g/100 g carbohydrate, 2.2 g/100 g fibre); HPB does not publish a separate sugar field for this entry. 2. saturated_fat_g (2.1 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for comparable deep-fried wheat dough products (USDA SR Legacy, 'Doughnuts, yeast-leavened, glazed' as secondary reference); HPB does not publish saturated fat breakdown for this entry. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions not reported in HPB Foodo for this entry; not available in USDA FoodData Central for an exact match. 4. potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient and moisture values are not reported in the HPB Foodo database for this entry and no exact-match approved source was identified as of 2026-04-17. 5. GI of 76 is an analogous estimate based on deep-fried wheat dough (Chinese cruller/youtiao) from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for ham chim peng with red bean has been published in an approved source. 6. Dietary tag 'plant_based' preserved from input; confirmed as vegetarian and vegan in traditional preparation without eggs or dairy. 7. 'Haam Jin Beng red bean' and 'Haam Jin Beng (Cantonese)' confirmed present in flat aliases. Chinese entry '鹹煎餅（红豆）' added to both aliases_by_language chinese and flat aliases to ensure complete consistency.
